Organizations must configure those platform-level protections in addition to Avi's application-layer controls to satisfy the full scope of this control.

### Control 03.01.20.c.02

> Retaining approved system connection or processing agreements with the organizational entities hosting the external systems.

**SCF Controls:** DCH-13, DCH-13.1, DCH-14.2, DCH-14.3, DCH-18, NET-05, TPM-05

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF provides multiple Access Control List (ACL) mechanisms and infrastructure visualization capabilities across its component products that provide the technical foundation for data access mapping. These ACL capabilities define which users and groups have access to specific data stores and platform entities, providing the raw access control data from which a logical map of data-sharing relationships can be constructed. Full satisfaction of this control also requires organizational processes to aggregate and maintain the mapping outside VCF.

VCF Automation maintains an ACL table that contains entries defining the access users have to specific entities in the system. System administrators with the Manage General ACL right can create, modify, and remove specific ACLs through the accessControls API. The VCF Automation API can also be used to edit the ACL table for defined entity access control. This programmatic access to ACL data allows organizations to query and export access relationships for mapping purposes. VCF Automation also supports project-level access controls governed by predefined roles, giving administrators visibility into which users and groups can reach specific projects and their associated resources.

At the storage layer, vSAN file service supports managing ACLs on SMB shares using the MMC tool, giving administrators the ability to define and review which parties can access file-level data. In vSphere Supervisor environments, ACLs are used to isolate file share access within supervisor namespaces, restricting data access to authorized consumers within each namespace boundary.

VCF provides role-based access control structures that complement ACL-based mapping. In VMware vCenter, privileges are fine-grained access controls that can be grouped into roles and then mapped to users or groups, creating a traceable relationship between identities and the data or objects they can reach. VCF Operations supports group-based access control, allowing administrators to configure groups and assign roles to group members. VCF Operations Scopes further refine access control by associating defined scopes with user accounts and user groups. The vSphere Automation REST API supports data sets with attributes that define access control and interoperability configuration, including the ability to specify whether access is granted to individual users or groups through the subject type setting.

NSX Projects allow delegation of networking and security configuration management to specific sets of NSX users while restricting visibility to only project-scoped objects. This provides a natural boundary for documenting data-sharing relationships, since each project isolates networking and security configurations for a given department or organizational unit.

VCF Operations for Networks provides network topology mapping capabilities that can diagram live system data flows and display paths between specified entities, including an end-to-end interactive topology map covering physical and virtual devices. The application topology map represents flows between network entities, helping teams visualize the relationships between workloads, systems, and connected services. The databus framework in VCF Operations for Networks supports sharing stored data with other applications through APIs, enabling high-volume, low-latency data export. These data flows can be documented as part of an access mapping exercise. VCF Operations also provides a relationship map that displays objects in the context of parent and child relationships, functioning as a hierarchical navigation system for understanding access and connectivity relationships.

To fully satisfy this control, organizations need to aggregate ACL and role-mapping data from across VCF components into a consolidated logical map that identifies all parties with whom sensitive or regulated data is shared. VCF provides the underlying access control infrastructure, programmatic APIs, and topology visualization to extract and represent this information, but the creation and maintenance of the data access map, along with formal Interconnection Security Agreements, are organizational governance activities that typically require dedicated data governance or identity governance tooling to correlate access rights across the full environment.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend (vDefend) provides several mechanisms that contribute to governing how external parties and technology assets, applications, and services (TAAS) are used to store, process, and transmit data.

The vDefend distributed firewall (DFW) and Gateway Firewall (GFW) enforce network-level policies governing which external parties and external services are permitted to communicate with internal systems, restricting unauthorized data flows to and from external TAAS and third-party services.

Security Services Platform (SSP) maintains a centralized inventory of current organizational IT assets, including servers, software, and network devices along with their configurations. SSP classifies assets into infrastructure assets, regions, zones, environments, and applications, and identifies whether application assets are critical or regular. This classification gives administrators the context needed to define appropriate network-level controls on external data flows. Platform Inventory monitors asset operational state, collects VM tags with their scopes for asset tracking, and organizes assets by region and zone.

SSP manages certificate infrastructure for external connectivity. Web proxy certificates identify the proxy servers used to connect securely to external services. CA bundles establish trust for communicating with external services, including the Malware Prevention Service when enabled. SSP rejects certificates or configurations using deprecated cryptographic algorithms or weak key lengths for external connections. License Hub also supports importing CA bundles to establish trust for communicating with external services.

The Security Segmentation workflow in SSP identifies risky application protocols that transmit data in cleartext and recommends restricting or blocking them as part of segmentation policy. Administrators can review segmentation analysis, classify application assets as critical or regular, and publish environment rules, inventory assets, and policies to enforce controls on external data flows.

vDefend TLS Inspection supports External TLS Decryption for traffic destined to external services the enterprise does not own, making decrypted traffic available for IDS/IPS, Malware Prevention, and URL Filtering inspection.

vDefend does not directly govern contractual or legal obligations with external parties, but it enforces the network-layer technical controls that reflect those obligations.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) verifies that individuals and systems have requisite authorizations before transferring data between sites. PNR performs operations using the user credentials supplied when connecting the protected and recovery sites, and configuring replication on virtual machines requires permissions on both the local and remote VMware vCenter instances. After the sites are connected, permissions can be changed from the same PNR user interface on either site, and users or groups are selected from a domain and assigned a role to receive permissions in PNR.

The PNR import and export tool accepts the --remoteAuthUseSA, --remoteAuthSAPath, --localAuthUseSA, and --localAuthSAPath parameters to authenticate to the remote and local sites using Service Account files, providing non-interactive authorization for configuration data transfers. PNR service accounts have associated roles and privileges that must not be deleted or modified. Access to the PNR Appliance Management Interface requires authentication as the admin user for time zone, network, service start/stop/restart, and Storage Replication Adapter configuration.

vSphere Replication uses role-based access control with predefined roles that can be cloned and customized by adding or removing privileges based on organizational needs. vSAN Snapshots and Replication augments vCenter roles with additional permissions for VM operations such as restore and linked clone, including the VsanDPVirtualMachineAdministrator role assigned by a vCenter administrator through the vSphere Client. PNR for VCF Automation adds role-based permissions for Provider Administrators, Organization Administrators, Project Administrators, and Project Advanced Users, with Provider Administrators selecting which replication classes each Organization can consume. Namespace unpair operations require Organization Administrator role authorization, and administrator login is required to create protection groups, protection group folders, array-based replication protection groups, and to execute VCF Operations orchestrator workflows for PNR.

For isolated recovery scenarios, the Clean Room Orchestrator is authorized to send event and status data to a Data Collection Endpoint and Data Collection Rule through role-based access control assignment of the Monitoring Metrics Publisher role to an Entra Application, scoping which identities can move telemetry across the recovery boundary.

Defining authorization rules for cross-site data transfers, identifying which workloads require explicit transfer approval, and operating the transfer-authorization policy are organizational responsibilities. PNR supplies the dual-site permission, role, and service-account mechanisms; the organization decides who holds the corresponding roles and approves the transfers.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) contributes to this control through role-based access controls, identity integration, and connection-level authorization mechanisms that restrict data transfer operations to authenticated and authorized entities.

DSM provides two platform-level roles: DSM Admin and DSM User. These roles govern what operations a user may perform, including provisioning databases and establishing replication relationships between instances. Users can be sourced from LDAP or LDAPS identity providers, or maintained as local accounts. When directory service integration is configured, DSM uses the configured directory service for both authentication and authorization. Administrators import LDAP users through the DSM Admin UI and assign them the DSM Admin or DSM User role. MySQL database instances can further delegate authentication to Active Directory via LDAPS. SQL Server databases support Windows Principal authentication, allowing users to authenticate with Active Directory credentials; administrators can optionally configure privileged Active Directory account credentials to activate automated Kerberos integration. Role assignment determines which users are permitted to initiate operations that involve transferring data, such as establishing replication or running migrations.

For client applications and services connecting to DSM-managed database clusters, DSM enforces certificate-based trust requirements before connections can be established. Custom trusted certificates used in DSM must comply with FIPS requirements. Database clusters issue certificates for client connectivity and monitoring connections. PostgreSQL databases support host-based authentication (HBA) configuration, with the default configuration requiring SCRAM-SHA-256 authentication for service accounts including postgres_exporter and pgadmin. When LDAPS users access PostgreSQL databases, administrators configure specific privilege grants, including database and schema-level access, before those users can perform operations. These mechanisms control which client systems and identities may establish connections and exchange data with the managed databases.

Data transfer operations between DSM database instances carry additional authorization requirements tied to role and identity verification. During PostgreSQL database migration, DSM requires TLS verification between source and target clusters. For multi-site PostgreSQL replication, users with the DSM User role must manually establish trust between primary and secondary instances before replication proceeds; users with the DSM Admin role have trust established automatically. This role distinction governs which identities may initiate and complete cross-instance data transfers. MySQL database migration to DSM requires creation of a dedicated user account with minimum privileges of BACKUP_ADMIN, REPLICATION_SLAVE, SELECT, and INSERT on the mysql schema, restricting the migration account to only those permissions the operation requires. DSM also verifies network access to the source database and validates SSL trust during MySQL replication operations.

For data forwarded to external systems, DSM requires authenticated connections before transfer proceeds. Metrics forwarding supports Mutual TLS (mTLS) authentication, configured by enabling the Client Certificate Authentication option and supplying the client certificate and private key. Forwarding to other metrics targets requires verification of server address, port, authentication type, credentials, and certificates before the forwarding connection is accepted. Webhook notification endpoints support basic authentication with Authorization User and Password credentials, bearer token authentication via Authorization header, or authorization embedded in the webhook URL for Slack-compatible endpoints. For self-service database consumption, the Consumption Operator requires a TLS certificate for secure communication with the DSM provider, and an authentication secret containing the credentials needed to connect to the DSM provider must be created and configured before the operator can connect.

DSM's authorization mechanisms primarily govern user-initiated operations and connection-level access within the database service layer. Verifying authorizations for all Technology Assets, Applications and/or Services transferring data across interconnected systems requires organizational data governance processes that extend beyond DSM's scope. Organizations must define which services are authorized to connect to DSM-managed databases, maintain records of authorized interconnections, and audit application-layer data flows independently of the platform controls DSM provides.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer (Avi) provides application-layer transfer authorization mechanisms that verify client and service credentials before forwarding data to backend resources. For application traffic, Avi's OAuth and OpenID Connect (OIDC) integration supports the Authorization Code Grant Flow, through which the authorization server authenticates the resource owner, evaluates the client's access request, and issues access tokens only after authorization is confirmed. Clients must present a valid access token to the Avi virtual service; the Authorization Endpoint handles user-agent redirection to the authorization server, and the token endpoint validates the authorization code and authenticates the client using the client_id and client_secret before tokens are issued. This flow verifies that the requesting party holds the required authorization before any data transfer to the backend resource server is permitted.

For more granular application-layer authorization, Avi supports Authorization Policies and DataScript within OAuth/OIDC flows. These allow administrators to define rules that evaluate token claims and request attributes, granting or denying forwarding based on whether the client meets the configured authorization criteria. This provides control over which clients and services have the write permissions or privileges required to access specific application endpoints.

For administrative operations on the Controller, Avi's role-based access control enforces object-level write permissions. Write access to objects such as Virtual Services, Application Profiles, SSL/TLS Certificates, and Certificate Management Profiles is granted at the role and tenant level, so that only users with the requisite permissions can modify transfer-related configurations. TACACS+ integration supports centralized authorization by delivering attribute-value pairs from the TACACS+ server that map users to specific roles and tenants. The PASS_ADD and PASS_REPL statuses grant authorization to successfully authenticated users, while the FAIL status rejects those without the required attributes. Cisco ISE can be configured as a remote authentication and authorization backend for Avi, including with external identity sources such as OpenLDAP, providing centralized identity management for Avi administrative access. In Avi Load Balancer SaaS deployments, authorization tokens can be generated for service account authentication to the SaaS Controller.

### Control 03.01.20.d

> Restrict the use of organization-controlled portable storage devices by authorized individuals on external systems.

**SCF Controls:** DCH-01.2, DCH-13, DCH-13.1, DCH-13.2, DCH-13.4, MDM-01, MDM-07

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF provides multiple layers of protection for sensitive and regulated data across storage, network, and application tiers within the platform.

At the storage layer, vSAN delivers both data-at-rest encryption and data-in-transit encryption. Data-at-rest encryption protects data on storage devices in the event a device is physically removed from the cluster, encrypting all data written to the vSAN datastore at the cluster level. Data-in-transit encryption protects data as it moves between hosts within the vSAN cluster. These capabilities operate at the infrastructure level, meaning all virtual machines and workloads hosted on vSAN-backed storage benefit from encryption without requiring changes to guest operating systems or applications. vSAN storage policies let administrators enforce encryption preferences per virtual machine, with options for required encryption, no encryption, or no preference (the default, which applies both encryption rules). Both vSAN encryption approaches require key management through either an external KMIP-compatible Key Management Server (KMS) or the vSphere Native Key Provider (NKP). The NKP is included in all vSphere editions and does not require an external key server; vCenter generates the Key Encryption Key and pushes it to the ESX hosts, which then generate Data Encryption Keys. vCenter file-based backups contain the Key Derivation Key used by the Native Key Provider and must be stored securely; the backup feature supports optional password-based encryption of the backup archive, which is required to complete any subsequent restore.

For non-vSAN storage (iSCSI, NFS, Fibre Channel), VM-level encryption protects all virtual machine files through storage policies that apply the vmwarevmcrypt encryption IO filter. Administrators create encryption storage policies by associating capability instances with rules through the PBM Profile Manager. vCenter exposes an extensive set of Cryptographic Operations privileges that allow fine-grained control over who can perform encryption tasks. This privilege model restricts encryption key management and cryptographic operations to authorized administrators, supporting separation of duties between storage administrators and security administrators. Storage security best practices within VCF guidance recommend securing access to storage devices, implementing storage encryption, and protecting virtual machine data. If virtual machines are not already encrypted, the platform guidance recommends enabling encryption to reduce the risk of data exposure.

For data in transit across the network, encrypted vSphere vMotion encrypts all sensitive data when virtual machines and data move across the network during live migration. For non-encrypted virtual machines, encrypted vMotion can be set to Opportunistic (use encryption if supported) or Required (do not migrate if unsupported), giving administrators control over migration security posture. Fault Tolerance log traffic, which replicates virtual machine memory state to a secondary host, can also be encrypted to protect sensitive data within the replication stream.

For sensitive data handled within platform automation and orchestration workflows, VCF provides additional safeguards. VCF Automation APIs support sensitive data protection through a dedicated masking notation for passwords and credentials used in machine configurations, image profiles, and projects. Sensitive values such as passwords and private keys are not stored or transmitted in plain text within automation workflows; only the encrypted representation is visible in API responses. VCF Operations orchestrator signs and encrypts packages to protect data handled by orchestration workflows. The platform also supports storing sensitive information such as credentials in orchestrator configuration elements or external password vaults, allowing organizations to use their existing secrets management infrastructure.

For containerized workloads consumed through VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) on the vSphere Supervisor, the platform provides Kubernetes-native mechanisms for protecting sensitive and regulated data. Kubernetes Secrets serve as the primary storage mechanism for credentials, tokens, and certificates within VKS clusters, with platform guidance recommending that sensitive data be stored in Secrets rather than container images, that applications avoid writing Secret values to nonvolatile storage or logging them in clear text, and that short-lived Secrets be used to limit the impact of unintended exposure. The Secret Store service, available as a Supervisor Service, stores credentials, tokens, and access keys as encrypted values and enforces role-based access control for administrators, DevOps engineers, and application tenants managing and distributing sensitive data. Organizations requiring integration with an external vault can use the Secrets Store Container Storage Interface (CSI) Driver to inject sensitive data managed in a provider such as HashiCorp Vault into workloads at runtime, rather than storing it as cluster-native Kubernetes Secrets; HashiCorp Vault is also used within the Harness Continuous Integration and Continuous Delivery Supervisor Service to manage and secure sensitive data including API keys, passwords, and certificates. Platform guidance recommends using storage integrations that provide encrypted volumes, or encrypting data within the application itself, to protect data written to filesystems mounted into containers. For high-assurance environments, cryptographic protections can be applied to audit logs to make log records tamper-resistant and confidential.

Pod Security Admission (PSA) provides namespace-level workload isolation for VKS clusters. The Restricted PSA standard, configurable per VKS namespace, enforces pod hardening practices that include restrictions on Linux capabilities, requirements for non-root execution, seccomp profile enforcement, and prohibition of privilege escalation, which restricts what a compromised container can access within the platform. VKS cluster backup and restore operations are managed through the VCF Automation Tenant Portal under Manage & Govern > VCF Services > Kubernetes Management, supporting backup of entire clusters, selected namespaces, or specific resources identified by label; backup repositories support encryption to protect workload data copies during storage.

VCF platform guidance explicitly warns against storing sensitive data such as passwords or private keys in plain text in data sets; sensitive values must be protected through the encryption and masking mechanisms the platform provides. Administrators should also be aware that vSAN support bundles may contain sensitive information, including personal, health care, or financial data, and that uploading support bundles to external destinations may expose regulated data. Organizations should evaluate support bundle contents before sharing them outside the environment.

All VCF components use FIPS 140-3 validated cryptographic modules. NSX cryptographic modules are validated through the NIST Cryptographic Module Validation Program (CMVP), and VCF Operations for Networks uses FIPS-validated modules as well. This validation applies across the platform's encryption capabilities, covering both data-at-rest and data-in-transit protections.

Classifying which data is sensitive or regulated, selecting which protection layers apply per data class, and operating the data-handling policy across customer workloads sit with the organization.

VMware Private AI Services (PAIS) addresses this control through a set of AI-specific data protection mechanisms that govern how models, training data, knowledge bases, and inference workloads are stored and accessed on VCF.

The Model Gallery uses Harbor project access capabilities to restrict access to sensitive data used to train and tune models. PAIS integrates with Harbor registries as the model storage backend, and model retrieval requires authentication and certificate-based trust, so only authorized clients can pull stored artifacts.

Access to PAIS itself is controlled through OIDC group membership. The authorizedGroups configuration limits which user populations can consume the service, scoping data access at the platform-entry layer. Programmatic access to Knowledge Bases and data sources is gated by personal access tokens generated from user account settings, which lets administrators issue and revoke scoped credentials for automated retrieval workflows.

Secure connections are required for all interactions between PAIS and external data sources. PAIS uses CA trust bundles for HTTPS connections to OIDC providers, Harbor registries, content management systems hosting knowledge database data, and MCP servers that supply tools to models. A separate CA trust bundle is configured for database connections to the pgvector server used by Retrieval-Augmented Generation, and a customer-supplied PostgreSQL database can be brought in through a "Bring your own PostgreSQL" option that accepts the database CA certificate when the issuer is self-signed. CA trust bundle management is also exposed at model-endpoint configuration time so that secure connections to external registries and content management systems can be established when running completion or embedding models. SharePoint On-Premises data sources for PAIS Knowledge Bases require user name and password authentication, which scopes data retrieval to credentials held by the platform.

GPU resources used to process AI workloads are isolated by tenant: GPU-capable VM classes with GPU reservations restrict a given GPU to a particular namespace, and reserved GPU VM classes can be confined to a single zone or supervisor for tenant isolation. This helps prevent inadvertent sharing of accelerator resources that may process sensitive inference traffic across tenant boundaries.

PAIS is compatible with OCI-compliant registries from other vendors and supports disconnected, air-gapped deployment topologies for environments where network-level data isolation is required.

Foundational data protection capabilities, including encryption at rest, VM-level isolation, identity management, and network-layer protection, are provided by the underlying VCF platform and by adjacent products such as VMware Data Services Manager and VMware vDefend. See VCF Coverage for those controls.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend contributes to sensitive and regulated data protection by enabling microsegmentation through the vDefend distributed firewall (DFW), which restricts unauthorized inter-application and intra-application-tier traffic to reduce the network attack surface around workloads that process sensitive data. The DFW enforces Layer 2 through Layer 7 access policies, including application identity-based and user identity-based controls and FQDN filtering, enabling organizations to define precise access restrictions around workloads handling regulated data. The DFW also supports targeted deny rules scoped to infrastructure groups such as DNS and DHCP servers through the Applied-To field, supporting phased adoption of least-privilege network access for critical infrastructure services.

Security Services Platform (SSP) extends this protection through several mechanisms. SSP Application Protection in Strict Mode enforces application-level firewall rules across all application workloads, with scoring based on workload coverage, traffic coverage, and lock-down policy adoption. SSP Environment Protection evaluates whether workloads are protected by environment-specific segmentation policies such as development, production, and test tiers, helping operators confirm that workloads handling regulated data operate within appropriately isolated zones. Security Intelligence within SSP enables administrators to enforce microsegmentation by converting observed allow flows to explicit deny rules after confirming no additional unprotected flows exist. SSP also identifies risky application protocols that transport data in clear text and recommends enforcing minimum secure protocol versions across internal communications, directly supporting protection of sensitive data in transit.

SSP's Segmentation Monitoring and Security Segmentation Score surface residual protection gaps. Segmentation Monitoring categorizes infrastructure services as fully protected, partially protected, or not protected, and provides recommendations for servers and services lacking full policy coverage. The Security Segmentation Score quantifies the number of unprotected traffic flows hitting default allow rules out of all unique flows associated with known risk factors, giving operators a measurable indicator of exposure across the environment.

Network Detection and Response (NDR) anonymizes sensitive customer information when sharing telemetry data to cloud services, protecting the confidentiality of data processed by the detection platform itself. vDefend does not provide data-at-rest encryption for workload storage or data classification capabilities; those controls remain the responsibility of VCF platform capabilities and organizational data management practices.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

VCF Protection and Recovery (PNR) contributes to protecting sensitive and regulated data by maintaining replicated copies at geographically separated sites and providing orchestrated recovery when primary copies are compromised or corrupted. Bidirectional protection is implemented by protecting datastore groups or storage policies using array-based replication, or by protecting individual virtual machines using vSphere Replication, allowing organizations to keep regulated workloads recoverable across paired sites.

Protection groups allow administrators to organize regulated workloads and apply consistent replication and snapshot policies across the virtual machines and datastores subject to the same data protection requirements. PNR protection groups support an immutability mode that, when enabled, prevents the data protection group from being modified or deleted, which addresses tamper resistance for the configuration that governs how regulated workloads are protected. For multi-tenant or shared recovery scenarios, PNR maps resources, networks, folders, and datastores on protected sites to separate datacenters, networks, folders, and placeholder datastores on the shared recovery site, preserving customer data isolation across the recovery boundary.

vSAN Snapshots and Replication provides VM restore and clone operations from existing snapshots, supporting recovery of regulated workloads to a known-good point in time. PNR records operational information into log files that do not contain sensitive information such as private keys or passwords, which limits the recovery tooling itself as a source of disclosure. The Protection and Recovery dashboards in VCF Operations provide cross-site visibility into protected and recovery sites, vSAN snapshot and replication state, and VMware Live Recovery Cloud regions, supporting monitoring of regulated workload protection.

Encryption at rest for primary storage, data classification, granular access controls at the data layer, and key management are provided by the underlying VCF platform rather than PNR itself.

#### VMware Data Services Manager (Contributes)

VMware Data Services Manager (DSM) contributes to the protection of sensitive and regulated data through several database-platform mechanisms covering data in transit, data retention, storage policy enforcement, tenant isolation, and application-level cryptographic capabilities.

For data in transit, DSM requires TLS connections to database clusters. Clients establish these connections by adding the relevant certificate authority to the OS trust store, or by retrieving the CA certificate from a custom certificate secret through the DSM API for automated workflows. Beginning with DSM, DSM enforces FIPS 140-3 compliant cryptographic standards and validates certificates during upload, rejecting unsupported algorithms or parameters. A TLS certificate for secure communication between the Consumption Operator and the DSM provider must be obtained and configured by the cloud administrator before enabling self-service consumption.

For backup and data retention, DSM includes automated backup capabilities with configurable schedules supporting both full and incremental backups. Database administrators configure backup storage locations and retention periods on a per-database basis. DSM retains automated backups of deleted databases until the configured retention period expires, supporting recovery from accidental deletion. Control plane backups are stored in S3-compatible object storage with a unique identifier tied to the control plane ID. DSM supports point-in-time recovery (PITR), restoring a backup at a specified point in time to a newly created database VM. PITR is accessible through both the DSM interface and the VCF Automation UI. If an incremental backup in a backup chain is deleted, DSM cannot restore data from later incremental backups in that chain, so maintaining a consistent backup schedule is important for recoverability.

For storage placement and access constraints, DSM infrastructure policies control which storage types and storage policies are available for database virtual machines and govern VM placement within the infrastructure. Storage policies in DSM determine the datastore placement of database VMs and filter available storage options to those compatible with the specified compute resource. Data service policies can be defined and enforced across tenants for MySQL, PostgreSQL, and SQL Server databases, including allowlists that restrict users to approved provisioning parameters. In multi-tenant deployments using VCF Automation, administrators can assign object stores and data service policies to specific tenants or organizations, enforcing isolation and resource constraints across database clusters.

For application-level data protection, PostgreSQL databases in DSM support the pgcrypto extension (version 1.3), which provides cryptographic functions that can be applied to secure specific data fields. DSM also stores its own internal user credentials encrypted using PGCrypto symmetric encryption with a dedicated encryption key. For SQL Server databases, DSM uses an internal management login that restricts user-defined triggers to a database-level security context, blocking access to server-level permissions during management operations.

Because this control asks whether mechanisms exist to protect sensitive and regulated data wherever it is processed or stored, DSM's contribution is at the database platform tier. The organization remains responsible for classifying what data is sensitive or regulated, configuring database-level access controls that restrict which authenticated users can read or write protected data, selecting storage and backup policies that satisfy encryption and data residency requirements for specific regulated categories, and maintaining a governance program that maps data classification to handling requirements.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer (Avi) provides application-layer mechanisms that contribute to protecting sensitive and regulated data in transit, at rest within its operational scope, and in application logs.

For data in transit, Avi Service Engines terminate TLS connections from clients, protecting sensitive application data from interception at the proxy boundary. The System-Secure-HTTP application profile secures HTTP traffic by enforcing HTTPS, protecting cookies, managing client IP visibility, and controlling protocol behavior; it is configured per virtual service under Templates > Profiles > Application in the Avi Controller UI. For scenarios requiring certificate-based back-end authentication, the Avi Kubernetes Operator's PKIProfile enables secure communication with back-end services through mutual certificate validation and encryption, configurable via Custom Resource Definitions in Kubernetes environments. The System-Standard SSL Profile provides SSL/TLS re-encryption for traffic to back-end servers, accessible under the virtual service configuration.

For sensitive data at rest within the Avi Controller, passwords and private keys are stored encrypted in a local database. Configuration backup files are protected by a passphrase scheme; restoring a backup requires supplying the passphrase used during the backup operation, so backup contents are not readable without it. The Avi Controller's FIPS 140-3 mode imposes stricter handling of keys, authentication data, and random number generation for deployments with federal compliance requirements. Backup and restore operations in FIPS mode verify that the FIPS configuration of the backup matches the target Controller environment, maintaining consistent security posture across recovery scenarios.

Avi provides controls to protect sensitive data in application logs. The Analytics Profile includes a sensitive log profile setting that governs how sensitive data is handled in client log records. Operators can configure masking or removal of sensitive HTTP headers such as cookies and authorization tokens from application log output, reducing the exposure of personally identifiable and authentication-related information in stored log data. These settings are configured in the Analytics Profile under the Load Balancing section of the Avi Controller UI.

The Avi Controller's RBAC model supports granular label-based permissions on cloud objects, enforced via the restrict_cloud_read_access field in controller properties. This restricts which users and roles can read cloud configuration objects that may contain sensitive credentials or settings.

When deploying or configuring Avi via Ansible, Ansible Vault can be used to securely store sensitive variables such as passwords and API tokens, keeping those credentials out of plain-text automation scripts.

In deployments integrated with VCF, VCF Operations is used to configure the external backup server and storage path for Avi Load Balancer backups. By default, Avi Load Balancer backups are stored on the same server where VCF Operations appliance backups reside, consolidating backup management within the VCF platform. Restoring an Avi Controller cluster from backup requires that the new instance remains synchronized with the VCF Operations lifecycle.

Protection of regulated data stored on Avi Controller and Service Engine VM disks at the infrastructure layer relies on VM encryption and vSAN encryption provided by VCF. Organizations must configure those platform-level protections in addition to Avi's application-layer controls to satisfy the full scope of this control.

### Control 03.01.22.a

> Train authorized individuals to ensure that publicly accessible information does not contain CUI.

**SCF Controls:** DCH-03.1, DCH-15, HRS-03, HRS-03.1, HRS-04.1, HRS-04.2, HRS-05, HRS-05.1, SAT-02, SAT-03, SAT-03.3, WEB-01

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF restricts the disclosure of sensitive and regulated data to authorized parties through layered access controls, data classification mechanisms, and audit capabilities that enforce need-to-know principles across the platform.

VCF SSO provides centralized authentication, and all components enforce role-based access control (RBAC) against that identity. VMware vCenter's authorization model maps users and groups to roles containing specific privilege sets and applies those mappings at the level of individual managed objects such as clusters, folders, resource pools, and virtual machines. This means administrators can scope access so that users see only the resources relevant to their responsibilities. The Datastore > Browse datastore privilege, which controls the ability to view, upload, and download files directly on datastores, should be assigned only to users or groups with a specific operational need, as broad access to this privilege can expose virtual machine data stored on shared datastores.

VCF Operations enforces privilege-based access control that restricts users without appropriate privileges from viewing collected data for managed objects. This limits visibility into operational metrics and configuration details on a per-object basis. Scope-based access control further limits what objects a user or group can see at all. VCF Operations also supports VCF SSO integration with an identity provider for centralized authentication across VCF components.

VCF Automation enforces granular entity-type access control, allowing administrators to grant access to specific entity types only to designated users and organizations. This restricts who can view or interact with particular resources, limiting data exposure to those with a legitimate need. Content sharing policies in VCF Automation support a hard enforcement mode that controls access to catalog items, restricting which users can discover and consume shared content. Where catalog items require additional governance, administrators can apply content sharing policies to those items with enforcement that helps prevent access by users outside the designated scope.

VCF Automation supports field-level access control on defined entity type properties, which can be marked as public, protected, or private. Public fields are visible to all users, while protected and private fields require elevated rights. Only users with Admin Full Control or both Full Control and a Full Control ACL can create and modify protected or private fields, restricting sensitive entity data to authorized administrators.

For sensitive data in provisioning workflows, VCF Automation allows data to be explicitly marked as sensitive using a dedicated prefix syntax. Marked data is encrypted by default, and only the encrypted form is visible in API responses, preventing inadvertent disclosure through the API. HTTP-REST plug-in objects treat sensitive information as internal and do not expose it through scripting properties; instead, sensitive values can be stored in VCF Operations Orchestrator configuration elements or delegated to external password vaults, keeping secrets out of general-purpose storage. The platform guidance for vSphere data sets explicitly directs administrators not to store sensitive data such as passwords or private keys in plain text.

VCF Automation and VCF Automation for VM Apps include a data compliance consent mechanism that must be explicitly enabled before the names of users are exposed through the platform. This setting provides administrators with a deliberate control point for restricting disclosure of personally identifiable information in support of data privacy requirements.

For cryptographic operations, vSphere provides a "No Cryptography Administrator" role that carries all Administrator privileges except Cryptographic Operations. Administrators can clone this role and create custom roles with only specific cryptographic privileges, such as allowing encryption but not decryption. This separation of duties restricts a single administrator from having unrestricted access to encrypted virtual machine data.

For Kubernetes workloads running on VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) and vSphere Supervisor, the platform provides a dedicated Secrets resource type for storing sensitive data such as credentials, tokens, and certificates, separating this data from ordinary configuration stored in ConfigMaps. Platform guidance directs operators to use Secrets rather than ConfigMaps for confidential data. vSphere Namespaces restrict access through three permission levels (Owner, Edit, and View) that map to Kubernetes ClusterRoles, limiting who can see or interact with containerized workload resources and their data. The Namespace Owner, Edit, and View roles are scoped to namespace-level resources, while broader vSphere Administrator and Supervisor Administrator roles control access to cluster-level and infrastructure objects. Namespace boundaries provide structural isolation: RBAC grants within one namespace do not extend to others, supporting the mapping of access domains to teams, applications, or data sensitivity tiers.

Kubernetes RBAC rules can be configured with least-privilege access to Secrets, restricting which users and service accounts can read or modify them. Because list and watch operations on Secrets return their full contents, platform guidance recommends restricting these permissions to only the most privileged system-level components and granting only get access where a component's normal behavior requires it. Granting broader list or watch access on Secrets constitutes a privilege escalation risk, as these operations expose Secret contents through list responses. Secrets can be mounted into specific containers within a Pod rather than being made available to all containers, scoping exposure to only those containers with a demonstrated need. Short-lived Secrets can limit the impact of inadvertent exposure. Administrators can configure audit rules to alert on specific events, such as concurrent reading of multiple Secrets by a single user, supporting detection of anomalous access patterns. When a Pod that depends on a Secret is deleted, the kubelet deletes its local copy of the confidential data. For environments with access control requirements beyond standard RBAC, such as restricting access to Secrets based on resource annotations, Kubernetes supports integration with third-party authorization mechanisms to enforce finer-grained policies. Platform guidance also directs that applications reading Secrets from environment variables or mounted volumes must not log those values in clear text or transmit them to untrusted parties, since the application itself becomes a potential disclosure point once it has read the value.

The Secret Store Service on vSphere Supervisor stores sensitive data such as credentials, tokens, and access keys as encrypted values, and enforces role-based access control for administrators, DevOps engineers, and application tenants when managing and distributing that data across the Supervisor environment. Secrets can be created, updated, and deleted through the VCF CLI or the Secrets Service page. For environments requiring external secret management, the platform supports integration with third-party Secrets store providers via the Kubernetes Secrets Store CSI driver, keeping sensitive data outside the cluster and granting workloads access through narrowly scoped credentials. The Argo CD Supervisor Service manages secrets securely outside of Git and enforces access controls across all CI/CD pipeline components; Harness CI/CD integration references HashiCorp Vault for managing API keys, passwords, and certificates within the pipeline.

Node isolation in multi-tenant workload environments limits the risk of information disclosure at the compute level: if a workload escapes a container, it has access only to containers and volumes mounted to that node. The Harbor Registry, available as a standard package for VKS clusters, enforces RBAC and artifact security policies to restrict access to container images and other build artifacts that may include sensitive content. The etcd datastore, which stores all Kubernetes Secrets, should be configured to limit access and not be publicly exposed.

Administrators should be aware that core files and audit logs generated by NSX may contain sensitive information such as passwords or encryption keys. Access to these artifacts should be restricted through operating system and network-level controls. Secrets held within the database of each vSphere Supervisor workload cluster are stored in clear text unless the Secret Store Service or encryption at rest is configured, which means that database access controls remain the primary mechanism for restricting disclosure of those secrets in the absence of additional controls. At the individual virtual machine level, setting the isolation.tools.setinfo.disable parameter to true helps prevent VMware Tools from passing information from guest operating systems to the hypervisor management plane, providing an additional mechanism to limit information disclosure from guest workloads.

VCF Operations Security Operations provides visibility into permission changes by logging user permission events with details including the event type, status, the user who initiated the change, a description, and a timestamp. This audit trail supports detection of unauthorized changes to access controls that could lead to improper disclosure of sensitive data.

Defining who has need-to-know, classifying which data is sensitive, and operating the role and entitlement policy that determines disclosure across the environment are organizational responsibilities.

VMware Private AI Services (PAIS) provides layered access control mechanisms across the AI infrastructure stack that restrict disclosure of model data, AI service endpoints, and configuration credentials to parties with authorized access.

The Model Gallery uses Harbor project access capabilities to restrict access to ML models and to the sensitive data used to train and tune those models. Project-scoped access defines which users and groups can pull from or push to the registry, giving administrators a primary control point for which artifacts each tenant can reach.

PAIS enforces service-level access control through OIDC group-based authorization. When activating PAIS in an organization namespace, administrators configure the authorizedGroups field with the identity provider group memberships that are required to reach PAIS endpoints. Only user accounts that belong to those configured groups can access PAIS. HTTPS-based communication with the OIDC provider is required, and PAIS uses YAML-formatted CA trust bundles to authenticate connections to the OIDC provider, Harbor registries, content management systems hosting knowledge data, and MCP servers that supply tools to models.

At the VCF Automation layer, PAIS catalog items are governed by project membership in VCF Automation. Secrets used by PAIS, such as private container registry passwords, are stored in VCF Automation under Administer > Certificates & Secrets > Secrets. The secret key and value cannot be viewed after creation, only updated, which restricts disclosure of credentials embedded in catalog items. The PAIS DSM database can also be scoped to be shared between developers and catalog users through a single toggle that creates a dedicated catalog item, giving administrators control over who can reach the vector store underlying retrieval workflows.

For knowledge bases used in Data Indexing and Retrieval, PAIS authenticates to external data sources such as Microsoft SharePoint on-premises sites and Amazon S3 compatible stores using credentials configured at data source creation time. These per-source credentials control which external content repositories PAIS can reach for indexing and model context. A knowledge base can be linked to a maximum of ten data sources, and data source types are fixed at creation, which limits the scope of repositories any single retrieval workflow can pull from.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend contributes to this control through several network-layer mechanisms that restrict the disclosure of sensitive data to authorized parties.

The Network Detection and Response (NDR) feature anonymizes sensitive customer information when sharing threat data to the cloud, limiting exposure of environment details to authorized channels. Organizations can further control whether threat telemetry leaves the environment through NDR data-sharing settings.

Security Intelligence identifies risky application protocols that transmit data in cleartext, such as HTTP, Telnet, FTP, TFTP, and LDAP, enabling security teams to detect and remediate unauthorized disclosure paths across the network. The Security Services Platform recommends that organizations restrict or block these cleartext protocols and enforce minimum secure versions of commonly used protocols, reducing the risk of data exposure through unprotected network flows.

The Security Services Platform defines an Auditor role that provides read-only access to view information and track activity without the ability to make changes, supporting least-privilege access to sensitive platform information. Platform role-based access controls restrict who can generate, modify, or view security recommendations and flow data.

Full restriction of unauthorized data disclosure extends beyond vDefend to encompass data loss prevention, endpoint controls, and data classification capabilities outside vDefend's scope.

#### VCF Protection and Recovery (Contributes)

Protection and Recovery (PNR) restricts disclosure of replicated and recovery data through role-based access controls applied at the protection and recovery layer. PNR access is controlled through role-based permissions assigned to Provider Administrators, Organization Administrators, Project Administrators, and Project Advanced Users, scoping who can view or modify protection groups, replication configuration, and recovery plans. Authorized administrators on both source and recovery sites must hold appropriate PNR roles before they can interact with protected workloads or initiate recovery actions.

PNR records operational information in log files that do not contain sensitive material such as private keys or passwords, reducing the risk of inadvertent disclosure through diagnostic output. The Protection and Recovery Global Console maintains a unidirectional data flow from PNR servers to VCF Operations, with VCF Operations not pushing information back to PNR servers or site pairs, keeping central monitoring isolated from the protected environment.

PNR generates permission events that record changes to PNR permissions, providing accountability for changes to the access controls that govern disclosure of recovery-protected data. Recovery plan execution details, including plan name, status, folder location, and mode of operation, are recorded, supporting after-the-fact review of who initiated recovery actions. Events are captured with timestamp, priority, message content, component name, severity level, configuration ID, component ID, and event type, supporting reconstruction of administrative actions on protected data.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer (Avi) contributes to this control through application-layer mechanisms that restrict which clients receive application data and how sensitive information is handled within Avi's own logs and analytics.

At the application access control layer, Avi's DataScript scripting engine supports credential-based authorization enforcement at the proxy boundary. DataScript can check incoming client credentials or user IDs against string groups configured with key-value pair whitelists and redirect unauthenticated users to a login page, enforcing need-to-know access at the point where requests enter the Avi Service Engine. DataScript also supports blocklist-based access control, checking client IP addresses against named IP groups and terminating connections from listed addresses.

Avi's tenant isolation model provides data plane separation between tenants. Tenants configured in Tenant Context receive both control plane and data plane isolation, scoping which workloads and data are accessible within each tenant boundary. Tenants in Provider Context receive control plane isolation only, which is a relevant consideration when planning multi-tenant deployments that carry sensitive application traffic.

Avi's controller RBAC limits administrative disclosure by restricting which users and teams can read configuration and operational data. Extended granular RBAC uses markers to restrict user permissions to specific resources based on label matching. Label-based permissions for cloud objects are enforced through the restrict_cloud_read_access field in controller properties, scoping which users can read cloud configuration data.

Avi supports OAuth and OIDC for client authentication, allowing applications to use delegated access without requiring users to share their credentials directly with third-party applications. The identity provider issues tokens to authorized applications, limiting credential exposure at the application layer.

Avi's Analytics Profile supports a sensitive log profile that controls how sensitive data appears in client-facing logs. Operators can configure this profile to mask or remove sensitive HTTP headers, including cookies and authorization tokens, from application log records, reducing the risk of exposing user credentials and identity data through log access. Avi also supports masking of personally identifiable information (PII) in application logs by removing or masking matched fields in URI and header content.

Avi's Central Licensing service collects only the data outlined in the Privacy document, with no additional data collected by or for the licensing service, which limits administrative data exposure through that channel.

Organizational data classification processes, data handling policies, and the broader platform-level access controls provided by VCF are also required to fully satisfy this control, which is why coverage is Contributes rather than Meets.

Not applicable for this control: VMware Data Services Manager. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of this product.

### Control 03.01.22.b

> Review the content on publicly accessible systems for CUI and remove such information, if discovered.

**SCF Controls:** DCH-15, MON-11, WEB-14

**Aggregate Coverage:** Contributes

#### VCF (Contributes)

VCF provides monitoring and auditing capabilities that contribute to detecting unauthorized access patterns and suspicious activity, though it does not include purpose-built data loss prevention or exfiltration detection mechanisms.

VCF Operations delivers event auditing that provides visibility into platform interaction changes across the VCF infrastructure and surfaces insights into suspicious access events and policy violations. Each audit event includes a detailed view containing information that helps analysts investigate suspicious user actions or system issues within the virtual infrastructure. Audit events capture interactions such as searches, logins, logouts, capability checks, and configuration modifications, creating audit records that can be reviewed for indicators of unauthorized activity. The audit data can be filtered by User ID, User Name, Auth Source, Session, Client IP, Category, and Message, allowing investigators to isolate activity by specific users or source addresses when reviewing potential disclosure incidents. Administrators can continuously monitor user security aspects to detect security risks early, respond to potential threats, and verify that the right users have the right level of access. VCF Operations monitors failed login attempts to reveal potential security threats such as brute-force attacks or users with incorrect credentials, and allows analysis of the frequency and source of failed logins.

The VCF Operations User Activity Audit report provides traceability of user activities including logging in, actions on clusters and nodes, changes to system passwords, activating certificates, and logging out. This audit tracking identifies who made changes to the data center during a specific range of time, which is relevant when investigating whether unauthorized access to non-public information occurred. The User Permissions Audit supports downloading user permissions audit information to a report in PDF or XLS format, enabling review of who holds access to sensitive resources. Auditing reports provide traceability of objects and users in the environment, supporting detection of unauthorized access to business-critical applications containing sensitive data.

VCF Operations Analytics can configure both static and dynamic thresholds to generate alerts when metric values deviate from expected baselines. Dynamic thresholding is enabled by default and activates early warning smart alerts. This behavioral alerting can flag unusual patterns such as unexpected access to sensitive resources, anomalous data movement, or activity from accounts outside normal usage patterns. VCF Operations includes a Potential Evidence tab that displays events, property changes, and anomalous metrics that may be relevant to a triggered alert, supporting correlation of anomalous behavior with specific alerts. When VCF Operations discovers new entities or detects configuration changes, it raises alerts that can indicate unauthorized modifications or resource provisioning. Alerts, dashboards, and the Security Operations dashboard provide interactive monitoring for these conditions.

VCF Operations for Networks captures administrative actions and login/logout alerts in its own audit logs, providing network-level visibility into administrative behavior. It also generates informational alerts when data source identity information such as a certificate or key has changed, providing an indicator of potential tampering or unauthorized modification. Admin role users can view and enable or disable Personally Identifiable Information in audit logs, providing controls over how sensitive data appears in monitoring outputs. VCF Automation generates audit logs that display status values or messages for each reportable action, including timestamps, user names, project names, and trace IDs.

For centralized monitoring, VCF Operations can send user activity audit logs to an external syslog server, enabling integration with a SIEM or log management platform where correlation rules and exfiltration detection analytics can be applied. This syslog forwarding also enables correlation of VCF audit data with broader network telemetry for exfiltration pattern detection. At the VMware ESX host level, audit records conform to RFC 5424 and contain event-based traceability information including the event time, status, description, and user; ESX hosts can transmit these audit messages directly to syslog collectors, extending host-level security activity coverage to the same centralized log infrastructure.

While VCF provides the foundational audit logging, event correlation, behavioral alerting, and log forwarding infrastructure needed to support information disclosure monitoring, organizations must implement additional tools and processes to fully address this control. Network-level traffic inspection and content-aware data loss prevention require products such as VMware vDefend or third-party DLP solutions. For deeper network-level exfiltration detection, VCF integrates with vDefend, which provides traffic analysis, deep packet inspection, and network detection and response capabilities. Defining what constitutes non-public information, establishing exfiltration detection rules within a SIEM, and maintaining an incident response process for disclosure events are organizational responsibilities that fall outside the platform's scope.

#### VMware vDefend (Contributes)

VMware vDefend's Gateway Firewall provides network boundary controls that restrict inbound and outbound traffic to and from publicly accessible systems, limiting network exposure at the perimeter. URL filtering applies access control based on URL categories, URL reputation, and custom URL lists, enabling policy-based governance over what external services and web content internal systems can reach.

For public web server traffic, IDS/IPS within VMware vDefend includes explicit guidance that such traffic be configured with Prevent or Detect action and never exempted from inspection, treating it as untrusted traffic that requires full IDS/IPS coverage. The Distributed Firewall (DFW) supports App Strategy, an intent-based security mode that allows administrators to select predefined strategies such as "Allow Outgoing Traffic" or "VPC Isolation" to automatically generate read-only system rules, reducing the risk of misconfiguration for publicly exposed workloads. The DFW also supports FQDN-based filtering through allowlist and denylist rules configurable with source groups, destination groups, and DNS context profiles, enabling domain-name-based policy enforcement for traffic to and from publicly reachable systems.

The Gateway Firewall supports inter-project isolation by denying all inter-project communication except for essential infrastructure protocols such as DHCP, DNS, NTP, and ICMP, helping to contain publicly accessible workloads within defined network boundaries. The Security Services Platform supports publishing environment rules, inventory assets, and policies as distinct operations that can be applied individually or collectively across infrastructure tiers, enabling segmentation of publicly exposed workloads from the broader environment.

VMware vDefend addresses network-layer controls only and does not govern web application content, content management system publishing workflows, or organizational review processes for publicly accessible information.

#### VMware Avi Load Balancer (Contributes)

VMware Avi Load Balancer provides enforcement mechanisms at the virtual service level that organizations can use to control access to publicly facing application endpoints. Network security policies applied directly to virtual service VIPs establish the front-end security boundary for any Avi virtual service exposed to the internet, allowing administrators to define which source addresses, protocols, and connection types are permitted through the Service Engine before requests reach backend workloads.

At the HTTP application layer, Avi's HTTP security policies provide rule-based request control. Rules can match on request attributes and enforce blocking actions, such as returning an HTTP 403 response with a custom response body, configured via the http_security_action_send_response setting. This allows Avi to block specific classes of traffic at the load-balancer boundary before requests reach application servers. Content rewrite profiles, associated with HTTP(S) virtual services through the content-rewrite-profile setting, enable modification of HTML content within request and response bodies, providing an additional mechanism to manage application-layer content delivery.

For globally distributed deployments using Avi's Global Server Load Balancing (GSLB), Avi includes settings to explicitly distinguish public from private service exposure. GSLB Client Groups, configured under Infrastructure > GSLB > Sites, classify clients as public or private to drive routing and access decisions. Client classification is determined solely by these Client Groups. In NAT-aware public-private GSLB configurations, the private-list setting designates known private client addresses so that all unlisted addresses are treated as public by default. A separate publish_vip_to_public_zone flag in the cloud configuration controls whether a virtual service VIP with a private IP is published into a public DNS zone, providing a discrete toggle for controlling internet-facing DNS exposure of Avi services.

Organizations must define which content or services should be publicly accessible, classify data accordingly, and configure Avi policies to enforce those decisions. Avi provides the technical enforcement layer; content governance policies and configuration management remain organizational responsibilities.

Not applicable for this control: VCF Protection and Recovery, VMware Data Services Manager. This control addresses an end user activity or process, a design decision, or activity outside the scope of these products.

### Control 03.03.01.a

> Specify the following event types selected for logging within the system: [Assignment: organization-defined event types].

**SCF Controls:** MON-01, MON-01.4, MON-02.7, MON-03, MON-03.2

**Aggregate Coverage:** Meets

#### VCF (Meets)

VCF provides enterprise-wide monitoring through integrated tools that span infrastructure health, performance metrics, compliance assessment, log analysis, and alerting.

VCF Operations continuously monitors the operational state of all VCF components to detect conditions that may affect availability or compliance. It collects metrics across compute, storage, and networking resources, and correlates them over time to identify trends that may lead to potential failures. VCF Health, a component of VCF Operations, continuously monitors the operational state of VCF components to verify they are functioning as expected, giving platform operators a single view of operational status. Platform operators can configure alerts, perform log analysis, run infrastructure diagnostics, and review VCF health from a unified interface.

VCF Operations provides continuous compliance management by monitoring infrastructure against industry-standard benchmarks and custom policies. Administrators can create custom compliance benchmarks that monitor selected alert definitions and policies, tailoring the monitoring posture to organizational requirements. The platform assesses objects based on selected compliance policies and activates alert definitions associated with the current scorecard, providing ongoing visibility into compliance status. Compliance-related alerts are triggered on objects and displayed through a dedicated compliance alert subtype.

The alerting subsystem in VCF Operations supports the full lifecycle of alert management. Administrators can configure and manage alert definitions to monitor infrastructure events and conditions, including performance metrics, log events, and custom conditions configured through the Management Pack Builder. The Platform Health Alerts page provides a centralized view of all alerts related to overall system health, including alerts from data sources and infrastructure nodes. Health alerts are generated for conditions that affect the health of the environment and require immediate attention. Administrators can create policies to govern how alert definitions are evaluated, including which objects to monitor and what thresholds to apply, and can apply consistent practices to optimize alert behavior across monitored objects.

VCF Operations supports flexible grouping and scheduling to align monitoring with operational realities. Custom groups can be configured with automatic membership criteria to support monitoring strategies and achieve a high level of automation. Maintenance schedules can be assigned to objects undergoing planned downtime to keep monitoring data accurate and avoid false alerts when an object is offline for scheduled maintenance.

VCF Log Management provides centralized log collection and analysis. It ingests logs from VMware vCenter, VMware ESX hosts, NSX, vSAN, VCF Automation, VCF Operations, VCF Identity Broker, VCF Operations for Networks, and VCF Operations HCX. VCF Operations and VCF Log Management must be integrated to implement the centralized log collection architecture. The platform provides dashboards and analytics for troubleshooting and security investigation. Log-based alert definitions allow administrators to trigger notifications when specific log patterns appear across monitored systems. Log forwarding to external SIEM or syslog targets is configurable, with support for TLS encryption to protect log data in transit. The vCenter syslog forwarding API supports TCP, UDP, TLS, and RELP protocols. NSX Manager syslog supports TLS and requires port 6514 for encrypted log transmission.

At the vSphere layer, the alarm infrastructure integrates with events and performance counters to provide real-time alerting. The MetricAlarmExpression data object allows administrators to set alarms on specific performance metrics with configurable monitoring intervals and aggregation functions for processing data points. The vSphere Client provides direct visibility into tasks and events for change tracking and troubleshooting. VCF ships with preconfigured vSphere alarms that are active by default and monitor specific components including the Identity Management Service, Message Bus Configuration Service, ESX Agent Manager, VMware Service Control Agent, vSphere HA host status, and host connection and power state. The vCenter Management Interface provides appliance-level health visibility through an Overall Health badge that reflects one of five states (Good, Warning, Alert, Critical, or Unknown) across all vCenter components, and notifies administrators of available security patches through Critical or Alert badge indicators when automatic patch checks are enabled.

When VCF Automation is integrated with VCF Operations, the Insights dashboard and Alerts tab provide real-time capacity awareness and alert information for VCF Automation objects, giving IT teams visibility into resource usage across both provisioned workloads and the underlying infrastructure. The VCF Automation Monitor tab surfaces deployment health information sourced from VCF Operations, and deployment cards display Last Request Status information that tracks the most recent operation on each deployment (Create, Update, or Delete) and its outcome (Successful, In Progress, or Failed). The Management Pack Builder allows administrators to set up event definitions for integrated systems, monitoring specific conditions or thresholds and configuring alerts for proactive management. Management Packs extend monitoring to network devices, including sensor monitoring for devices such as switches and routers. Adapters can be installed on VCF Operations nodes to extend monitoring to additional systems and data sources beyond the core VCF components.

The VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) layer of VCF extends enterprise monitoring to container workloads and Kubernetes infrastructure. VKS clusters support MachineHealthCheck objects for both control plane nodes and worker node deployments; administrators manage these through the VCF CLI vcf cluster machinehealthcheck command, which supports configuration of unhealthy-condition definitions, maximum unhealthy thresholds (expressed as an absolute number or a percentage), and label selectors to target specific machines. The VCF CLI vcf cluster machinehealthcheck node get and vcf cluster machinehealthcheck control-plane get commands retrieve node and control plane health status on demand. VKS cluster health conditions, including the Ready and ControlPlaneReady status indicators, reflect the operational state of each cluster and are accessible via kubectl and the VCF CLI. The VCF Automation Provider Management UI includes a Services overview interface that exposes VKS cluster management functions and the health status of all cluster elements, giving platform operators a centralized view of the VKS tier. The Kubernetes API server within VKS clusters exposes diagnostic and health check endpoints (/healthz, /livez, /readyz, /statusz, and /metrics) that external monitoring systems can scrape to track API server availability and readiness; the kube-apiserver also emits the kubernetes_healthchecks_total counter and kubernetes_healthcheck gauge metric, which report structured health check status for components including etcd and autoregister-completion. Access to these monitoring endpoints is governed by the built-in Kubernetes system:monitoring RBAC group, which grants read access to liveness, readiness, and metrics endpoints while restricting broader cluster access. The Kubernetes Watch API allows monitoring tools to track changes to any API object as a live stream, supporting event-driven monitoring without continuous polling. The Node Problem Detector monitors node health and surfaces node-level problems as Kubernetes events and node conditions that external monitoring systems can consume via the event stream.

For workload-level monitoring within VKS clusters, Prometheus Operator is available as a standard package and defines custom resource definitions to deploy and manage Prometheus and Alertmanager StatefulSets, enabling high-availability alerting configurations. The Prometheus Alertmanager custom resource definition configures alert grouping, inhibition, and routing to external notification systems. The Istio service mesh, also available as a standard package on VKS clusters, provides service-level monitoring alongside its traffic management capabilities. The Dynatrace Operator can be deployed on VKS clusters to automate the deployment, scaling, and maintenance of the Dynatrace monitoring stack. Historical analysis, dashboarding, and alerting on cluster-level metrics require a dedicated monitoring solution configured to scrape the VKS metrics endpoints and subscribe to the Kubernetes event stream. The vcf cluster support-bundler create command gathers diagnostic information from Supervisor and VKS clusters when troubleshooting is needed.

At the network layer, NSX provides monitored indicators including node uptime status, deployment status, management and controller connections, tunnel status, PNIC status, and uptime duration. NSX time series APIs allow viewing data such as VPN statistics, including metadata about the number of tunnels configured and how many are up or down at a given time.

VCF Operations also supports self-monitoring through the VCF Operations adapter, which collects internal metrics such as activity success counts. This allows administrators to verify that the monitoring platform itself is functioning correctly. The VCF Operations policy engine monitors conditions and generates events to react to changing states in the orchestrator server or connected technologies. The Adapter Instance monitoring interval is configurable, with intervals as granular as five minutes.
